claim: Stated position: for rolling out the single medical information system
quote: Definitely for it, I even said it out loud at the general meeting back in June, that I'm ready to be in the pilot group if needed.

claim: Official reason for the position: reducing risk of errors and transparency for the patient
quote: Yes, reducing the risk, and honestly, transparency for the patient too, right now someone calls and they can't be told right away when their appointment is, because the three sources don't match up.

claim: Who else is for it: the chief physician and Marina from reception
quote: Of those clearly for it — the chief physician, of course, it's his initiative, also Marina from reception, she's long been tired of paper.

claim: Who is against it: head of surgery Artyom Viktorovich is unhappy about oversight from above
quote: Nobody's openly against it yet, but I know the head of surgery, Artyom Viktorovich, grumbles in private that it's just extra control from above.

claim: Suspicion of hidden disagreement among some department heads (hypothesis based on nonverbal signals)
quote: honestly I suspect maybe three out of five aren't happy deep down, but nobody's said it out loud, I'm judging by faces at meetings, not by words.

claim: Past experience: the 2022 electronic queue rollout failed, quietly shut down without explanation
quote: We tried, yeah, back in twenty-two we tried to set up an electronic queue, it was a disaster honestly, three months of everything crashing, doctors manually duplicating it on paper in parallel, then the system was quietly shut down, and nobody apologized, it was just like it never happened.

claim: Effect of the past failure on trust in new rollouts overall
quote: people now default to not believing things will get finished, we even got a saying, "we'll survive this one too," not maliciously, but there's a residue, and for me too, no point hiding it.

claim: Conditions for support: lossless data migration, proper training, reasonable access hierarchy
quote: Well, first, that the data from the old system gets transferred without loss, or it'll be like in twenty-two. Second, that there's proper training, not a two-hour webinar just for show. And third... well third, that access rights get set up sensibly, so not everyone sees absolutely everything, some reasonable hierarchy needs to stay in place.

claim: Personal loss: sole control over the schedule will become transparent to everyone, harder to make informal rearrangements
quote: it'll be a bit less convenient for me to distribute shifts the way I think best without extra questions, right now if I need to shift someone quietly, I just do it, and there it'll all be visible and, I guess, I'll have to explain it every time.

claim: Personal gain: less routine running around and calls, plus a bonus for the pilot rollout
quote: I definitely gain less running around, fewer calls of "where's my appointment," that's genuinely gotten old, plus, they say, management promised a bonus to whichever department head rolls it out first without hiccups, that's nice too, no point hiding it.

claim: Channel of influence: chief physician's biweekly planning meetings plus direct access to the chief physician
quote: Most often at the chief physician's planning meetings, they happen every two weeks, all the department heads gather there, I usually drop a thought carefully, not head-on, more like a suggestion to think about.

claim: HYPOTHESIS: the public position of "for transparency" conflicts with a hidden interest in keeping sole control over the department schedule
quote: in a sense that's my lever for keeping the department in order.

claim: HYPOTHESIS supported by direct admission of discomfort at losing informal control while declaring support for transparency
quote: I'm for transparency in general, but... well yeah, if I'm completely honest, it'll be a bit less convenient for me to distribute shifts the way I think best without extra questions

claim: Coalition map (from one interview): for — chief physician, Marina; opposed in private — Artyom Viktorovich; waiting it out — most department heads and doctor Olga
quote: "well, we'll see," you know, the kind of "we'll see" that actually means "I don't like this," but she didn't object outright.

claim: Respondent's influence power is high: regular channel via planning meetings plus a decade-long trusted personal relationship with the chief physician
quote: and also the chief physician trusts me, we've worked together ten years, so if something's important, I can go straight to him too, off the record.

claim: Conditional support is partly concrete (data migration, training) and partly vague ("reasonable hierarchy" — the respondent himself cannot pin down the boundary)
quote: I don't know how to put it right, but I feel total transparency is overkill.

claim: Risk of repeating the past failure is reduced on the budget front, but staff distrust about follow-through persists
quote: Better, because this time the budget is set for two years ahead, not just a quarter, I checked that separately with the CFO, specifically asked, so as not to walk into a second failure.

claim: Main insight: the respondent supports the system because of the real risk from three mismatched schedule sources, but the likely real interest is preserving control over shift allocation; influence is high via personal access to the chief physician; risk — hidden resistance from some department heads and doctor Olga may not surface until the pilot
quote: I'm for rolling out a single medical information system, because three sources for the schedule is a risk and a mess, but I probably do genuinely worry a bit about my own autonomy in scheduling shifts, and I want the new system to keep reasonable access boundaries, not make everything visible to absolutely everyone.
